coredata.dc:title: Focusing on the interfacial charge transfer by carbon quantum dots from FeTiO3 perovskite for enhanced photocatalytic antibiotic degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Simsek, Esra Bilgin
Tuna, Özlem
coredata.dc:description: Herein, FeTiO3 perovskite particles were functionalized with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using ultrasonication followed by impregnation methods. The functionalization enhanced visible light-driven degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) antibiotic via extended light absorption, promoted separation rate of photogenerated carrier and increased oxygen vacancies. The FeTiO3/CQDs composite including 20 mL of CQDs degraded 68.4 % of CIP within 120 min whereas FeTiO3 itself only degraded CIP by 36.5 %. On the basis of the structural characterization, the successful introduction of CQDs was confirmed in the FeTiO3/CQDs composite. The increased ratio of oxygen species was proved by XPS and ESR measurements, and the improved charge transfer was supported by PL analysis. The degradation mechanism was identified via scavenger tests; super oxide radicals and holes were found dominant in the degradation reaction. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance was boosted by adding two Fenton reagents to the reaction environment. The FeTiO3/CQDs composite catalyst also exhibited an excellent reusability and stability features. Accordingly, this study presented a novel and low-cost hybrid semiconductor as efficient and sustainable material for environmental purification under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Focusing on the interfacial charge transfer by carbon quantum dots from FeTiO3 perovskite for enhanced photocatalytic antibiotic degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, FeTiO3 perovskite particles were functionalized with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using ultrasonication followed by impregnation methods. The functionalization enhanced visible light-driven degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP) antibiotic via extended light absorption, promoted separation rate of photogenerated carrier and increased oxygen vacancies. The FeTiO3/CQDs composite including 20 mL of CQDs degraded 68.4 % of CIP within 120 min whereas FeTiO3 itself only degraded CIP by 36.5 %. On the basis of the structural characterization, the successful introduction of CQDs was confirmed in the FeTiO3/CQDs composite. The increased ratio of oxygen species was proved by XPS and ESR measurements, and the improved charge transfer was supported by PL analysis. The degradation mechanism was identified via scavenger tests; super oxide radicals and holes were found dominant in the degradation reaction. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance was boosted by adding two Fenton reagents to the reaction environment. The FeTiO3/CQDs composite catalyst also exhibited an excellent reusability and stability features. Accordingly, this study presented a novel and low-cost hybrid semiconductor as efficient and sustainable material for environmental purification under visible light irradiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Floroquinolone antibiotics are important groups of synthetic broad-spectrum antibacterial drugs which have been extensively used to treat diseases [1]. Ciprofloxacin (CIP), a kind of fluorinated quinolones, prevents cell replication by blocking the DNA replication pathway through targeting DNA gyrase of bacteria [2]. Due to the stable nature of CIP, it is partially metabolized and cannot be completely absorbed after entering the organism [3]. The residual CIP can accumulate gradually in the water environment, and it has been widely detected in the influent of wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) [4]. The conventional wastewater treatment techniques cannot efficiently remove the most of the pharmaceuticals, resulting in increased contamination to natural rivers and ground water [5]. For example, Al Aukidy et al. [6] monitored the release of pharmaceuticals in effluents from two WWTPs in Italy and concluded that 12 pharmaceuticals including ciprofloxacin were frequently detected in the effluents. In recent research in China [7], it was reported that the ciprofloxacin posed the greatest risk in aquatic environment among many pharmaceuticals. In Saudi Arabia, ciprofloxacin the most frequently detected molecule in the influent and effluent streams at high concentrations and it had the highest mass load in the effluent streams of selected three WWTPs [8]. Moreover, ciprofloxacin-resistant bacteria was detected as the highest abundances in soil, lake sediments, marine sediments in Fildes Peninsula, Antarctica [9]. On the other hand, ciprofloxacin concentration in European rivers were detected in the range of 100–1000 ng/L at many locations over the period 2003–2013 [2]. Therefore, development of effective and eco-friendly strategies for CIP removal is imminently needed.

On account of practical operation, low cost, environmental friendliness, photocatalytic remediation driven by sunlight utilization has been considered as one of the most auspicious methods to degrade the persistent pharmaceuticals from water environment. The choice of semiconductor is a key factor for excellent photocatalytic performance. Among the numerous semiconductors, perovskites have gained interest due to their excellent characteristics such as high chemical stability, redox behavior, ferroelectricity, piezoelectricity, and they have been generally applied in environmental remediation processes. Perovskites generally exist in the oxide forms as ABO3, with A site an alkaline or rare earth metal and B site mostly transition metals. Titanate perovskites (ATiO3) have been widely investigated by researchers due to their extraordinary stability and durability [10,11]. In the structure of ATiO3, Ti as the B-site transition metal has boundary d-orbitals with intimate energy to the O2p orbitals and thus, the electronic structure of titanate perovskites can be regulated by the orbital's hybridization near the Fermi energy level [10]. In general, titanate perovskites with wide band gap energy (> 3.0 eV) such as CaTiO3, SrTiO3, BaTiO3 and MnTiO3 have been extensively explored for their good catalytic activity. However, very limited number of titanate perovskites (ATiO3, A = Co, Fe, Pb, Cd, Ni) have absorption ability in the visible region. Among them, ferrite titanate-based perovskites (FeTiO3) have attracted a lot of attention in view of their low cost, excellent chemical stability, and environmental friendliness. FeTiO3 has a narrow band gap energy (2.5–2.9 eV), but its photocatalytic activity is limited due to its band potentials. Additionally, the high recombination rate of FeTiO3 restricts its utilization in the field of photocatalysis [12]. To overcome these drawbacks, several strategies such as doping [12], coupling with other semiconductors [13–16] or preparing composites with carbon based materials [17,18] have been developed by researchers. However, there has been no study reported about the incorporation of quantum sized materials with the FeTiO3 structure to improve its optical and photocatalytic properties.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new quasi-spherical type of carbon materials with diameters <10 nm, have many excellent features such as diverse raw material source, high electrical conductivity, good solubility, and great optical characteristics [19,20]. CQDs manifest greater photo-stability against decolorization and known as environmentally benign materials in contrast to heavy metal-based quantum dots (e.g. CdSe, CdS, ZnS) [21]. In particular, CQDs are known as excellent extractors of electrons, and they can boost the transfer rate of electrons [22]. CQDs can also be used as a solid-state electron migration medium between two semiconductors to enhance their catalytic ability. On the other hand, due to their unique up-conversion photoluminescence ability, CQDs enhance the utilization efficiency of sunlight and facilitate the transfer separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers [23]. The π-conjugated structure and oxygen-containing groups of carbon quantum dots provide intimate interaction with other materials to form stable hybrid materials [20]. In addition, the extended π-conjugated domains of CQDs can build strong π-π interactions with the organic pollutants, improving adsorptive and photocatalytic removal [24]. Therefore, in recent years, CQDs have been considered as potential candidates as modifiers to enhance the photocatalytic performances of semiconductors via tuning their optical and electrical properties. So far, CQDs have been widely introduced into traditional semiconductors to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [25,26], ZnO [27], g-C3N4 [23,28], Bi2WO6 [29] and other materials. Among them, a few studies focused on the modification of perovskites with CQDs such as LaFeO3/CQDs [30,31], CQDs/BaTiO3 [32], SrTiO3-, SnSrO3-, AgTaO3-CQDs [33]. Paszkiewicz-Gawron et al. [33] investigated the modification of wide band gap perovskites (SrTiO3, SnSrO3, AgTaO3) by using carbon and graphene quantum dots and the resultant materials displayed enhanced light harvesting features with improved photocatalytic activity towards degradation of phenol. Sun et al. [30] utilized CQDs as interface medium between Bi-LaFeO3 and C3N4 to enhance electron migration and promote the separation efficiency of charge pairs. Manchala et al. [31] constructed LaFeO3/CdS/CQDs ternary heterojunction system and demonstrated that the incorporation of CQDs improved the light absorption ability and provided superior photocatalytic performance.

Inspired by this concept, for the first time, we highlighted functionalization of FeTiO3 perovskite with CQDs for effective photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. For this, a facile and low-cost impregnation technique was applied to fabricate FeTiO3/CQDs catalysts. The hybrid catalyst with the highest CQDs amount demonstrated remarkable higher photocatalytic performance towards degradation of ciprofloxacin antibiotic, which was superior to the pristine FeTiO3 catalyst. The hindered recombination rate of charge pairs enhanced the adsorptive removal, increased surface oxygen vacancies, together with the synergistic effect between CQDs and FeTiO3 contributed to the high photocatalytic activity.

Ciprofloxacin (98 %, C17H18FN3O3), as target pollutant, was purchased from Acros Organics. Iron(III) chloride (98 %, FeCl3•9H2O) and titanium (IV) isopropoxide (97 %, C12H28O4Ti) were supplied from Sigma Aldrich. Triethanolamine (99 %, (HOCH2CH2)3N) and ammonium (25 %, NH3OH) were purchased from Merck. For the synthesis of CQDs, citric acid monohydrate ((≥99 %, C6H8O7•H2O) was obtained from Merck. In radical trapping tests, ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (≥98.5 %, EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (70 %, IPA), benzoquinone (≥98 %, BQ), were supplied from Merck and Isolab. In photo-Fenton tests, hydrogen peroxide (97 %, H2O2) and potassium peroxymonosulfate (98 %, 2KHSO5·KHSO4·K2SO4, PMS, Sigma-Aldrich) were applied as Fenton agents. All other chemicals were obtained commercially and used as received.

FeTiO3 perovskite catalyst was prepared according to the previous reported method [12]. Briefly, titanium (IV) isopropoxide (1.2 mL) and triethanolamine (2.6 mL) were mixed in distilled water and magnetically stirred for 15 min (Solution I). At the same time, iron (III) chloride (1.12 g) was stirred in distilled water (10 mL) for 15 min (Solution II). Then, solution II was added drop wisely to solution I under magnetic stirring. The solution pH was increased to pH 10 by using 0.5 M NaOH. After, the solution was transferred to 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 220 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The obtained brown precipitates were collected, washed with distilled water, and dried at 70 °C in air. The powders were then calcined at 700 °C for 4 h. The synthesis procedure was schematically presented in Fig. 1 .

As shown in Fig. 1, a specific amount of citric acid monohydrate (1.26 g) was dissolved in distilled water (50 mL), and then the solution was mixed by using a mechanical stirrer for 30 min at 50 °C. In a following step, the synthesis mixture was thermally treated at 220 °C for
12 h by using a stainless-steel autoclave. Finally, the CQDs solution was obtained after the as-produced solution was filtered.

As depicted in Fig. 1, the ferrite-type photocatalyst was decorated with carbon-quantum dots by performing an ultrasonic method. In this procedure, firstly, 0.025 g of FeTiO3 was added into the CQDs solution (20 mL). The ultrasonic mixing was applied to obtain homogenous solution and then the mixture was continuously stirred for 48 h. Next, the heterostructure catalyst was obtained after filtration and dried at room temperature. The same procedure was also employed for different amounts of CQDs (5, 12 and 20 mL). Considering the amount of QDs solution, the as-synthesized photocatalyst were designated as FeTiO3/CQDs(I), FeTiO3/CQDs(II) and FeTiO3/CQDs(III) for the CQDs dosage of 5 mL, 12 mL and 20 mL, respectively.

X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD, Rigaku Smartlab) was performed to determine the crystal structure of the photocatalyst. To observe the morphology of the pristine FeTiO3 and quantum dots-decorated FeTiO3, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL 6510) were performed. Moreover, the same SEM instrument equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was utilized to obtain element mapping of the heterostructured sample). In order to examine the chemical composition of the as-prepared sample, a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer was performed to observe the data of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The UV–vis absorption behaviors were investigated using a UV–vis spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 750). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained from a fluorescence spectrometer (PMT Detector 850 V) with 325 nm excitation. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded by using Jeol electron spin resonance spectroscopy.

The photocatalytic activities of the synthesized catalysts were evaluated through degradation of ciprofloxacin antibiotic under visible light illumination (150 W Halogen lamps, λ > 400 nm). Briefly, 15 mg of catalyst was added into 30 mL CIP solution (10 mg/L, pH = 6.1). The reactor was placed at the central of a rectangular shaped cabinet with 10 cm of distance from four halogen lamps (150 W, OSRAM, Germany) with flux intensity of ⁓100 mW/cm2. The suspended solution was stirred in dark for 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, the suspension was irradiated for the photocatalytic reaction. The reaction temperature was controlled and maintained at 24–25 °C by cooling fans at the bottom of the cabinet. At defined time intervals, 3.0 mL of the solution was taken and filtered by syringe filter to determine the remained concentration of CIP using Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer at the wavelength of 270 nm. The experimental parameters, such as solution pH (3−10), initial CIP concentration (5–15 mg/L) and catalyst dosage (0.5–1.0 g/L) were also investigated. Moreover, the effects of presence of different oxidative agents namely PMS and H2O2 were investigated with different concentrations (3–10 mM). Furthermore, the contributions of active radical species, •O2 −, •OH, and h+, were estimated by adding BQ, IPA and EDTA-2Na to the reaction medium. The removal efficiencies were presented as the percentage of removal, and it was calculated by using the following equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)

In addition, the related kinetic reaction rate constant (k) was determined by using the first-order reaction dynamic model given in Eq. (2):

where A0 and A are presented as follows: the initial and final concentrations (mg/L) at t = 0 and t = irradiation time (120 min), respectively and k (min−1) represents the first-order rate constant.

X-ray diffraction method (XRD) was performed to examine the structural properties of the as-obtained catalysts (Fig. 2 ). In XRD pattern of FeTiO3, several main peaks were detected in the range of 20–70°. These peaks were belonged to (012), (104), (110), (13), (202), (11), (024), (116), (018), (124), (300), and (208) planes of rhombohedral FeTiO3 (JCPDS; 75–1206) [13]. After decoration by CQDs, the peaks of the main crystalline structure were mainly observed in the spectrum, providing the presence of bare FeTiO3 crystal phase in the heterostructure sample. Apart from the main peaks of FeTiO3, the new peak centered at 25° occurred, corresponding to the highly disordered carbon atoms of CQDs in the FeTiO3/CQDs structure [34]. On the other hand, the other evidence for the interconnection of CQDs with FeTiO3 was the splitting of the diffraction signal at 33.2°. Interestingly, the diffraction peak at 44° disappeared when the CQDs was added, which could be attributed to the rotation of the oxygen atoms, stating the vast atomic migration between the titanate planes during the rhombohedral formation stage [35,36]. It could be stated that FeTiO3/CQDs heterojunction was successfully synthesized by performing the hydrothermal method followed by mixing.

The morphology of the as-prepared photocatalysts was examined via SEM analysis. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a-c), both FeTiO3 and FeTiO3/CQDs catalysts presented irregular spherical shape. In Fig. 3(d), it was observed that the surface exhibited roughness, which could be attributed to the existence of carbon quantum dots. This surface feature could be the reason for improving light absorption ability and increasing ciprofloxacin adsorption rate in the FeTiO3/CQDs system as compared to the bare FeTiO3 catalyst [37]. In Fig. 3(d), the EDS spectrum of FeTiO3/CQDs catalyst indicated the existence of Fe, Ti, O, and C elements and the corresponding mapping images showed homogeneous distribution of all elements, revealing successful incorporation of CQDs into the FeTiO3 surface. In order to gain an insight into the structure of FeTiO3/CQDs, TEM and HRTEM images were also obtained, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 . As illustrated in Fig. 4(a, b), the bare FeTiO3 sample consisted of spherical particles while quantum sized carbon dots were clearly observed on the surface of FeTiO3 particles with good distribution ranging from 2 to 6nm. (Fig. 4(c-e)). In Fig. 4(f), the HRTEM image of FeTiO3/CQDs displayed that the quantum dots with well-matched lattice distance were immobilized on the FeTiO3 surface to build intimate contact between the materials.

XPS investigation was performed to further determine the valence states of components in FeTiO3/CQDs heterostructure. Fig. 5 depicted the XPS spectra emerging Fe 2p, Ti 2p and C 1 s in the composite photocatalyst. Fig. 5(b) represented the Fe 2p spectra, whereas two peaks located at the binding energies of 710.4 eV and 724.1 eV were ascribed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively [38]. In the Ti 2p spectrum of FeTiO3/CQDs (Fig. 5(c)), two obvious peaks were observed at around 458 eV and 463 eV, corresponding to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, thereby indicating the existence of Ti4+ oxidation state [39]. In terms of C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5(d)), two obvious peaks appeared at 284.8 eV and 288.8 eV were assigned to graphitic CC and C꞊O bonds, respectively, confirming that the CQDs were in-situ incorporated into the perovskite structure [40,41]. Moreover, the O 1 s spectra of FeTiO3 and FeTiO3/CQDs samples were compared in Fig. 6 (a, b). The spectrum of the bare FeTiO3 catalyst demonstrated two main peaks in the region of 528.5–529.3 eV which were ascribed to the surface lattice oxygen (Ti–O–Ti) [42]. On the other hand, the O 1 s spectrum of FeTiO3/CQDs (Fig. 6(b)) was deconvoluted into three prominent peaks with binding energies of 529.1eV, 531.0 eV and 532.5 eV. The peak at 529.1eV in the spectra was associated with the lattice oxygen bond in the structure of FeTiO3. Furthermore, the adsorbed O2 was identified with the peak at 531.0 eV, representing oxygen vacancies in the heterostructure catalyst. In addition, the peak appeared at 532.5 eV was assigned to the existence of hydroxyl radicals resulted from the adsorbed H2O molecules. In order to confirm the XPS results, ESR analysis was conducted to check the oxygen vacancies in the FeTiO3/CQDs structure. As shown in Fig. 6(c, d), both samples showed symmetrical ESR signals (g = 2.004) which was related to the surface oxygen vacancies [43]. In case of FeTiO3/CQDs sample, the resonance peak of unpaired electrons was higher than that of the pristine FeTiO3, which could be resulted from enhanced amounts of oxygen vacancies [44]. Both XPS and ESR measurements demonstrated that the incorporation of CQDs increased the number of oxygen vacancies in the photocatalytic system, expediting catalytic activity and the situation was schematically presented in Fig. 6(e).

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was obtained to determine the light absorption features and band structure of the as-obtained catalysts. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the absorption edge of the pristine FeTiO3 was 595 nm, which resulted from the intrinsic light absorption ability. After the incorporation of CQDs into the structure of FeTiO3, the absorption edge was shifted in red region, that could be assigned to the interface interaction between FeTiO3 and CQDs. In case of the heterostructure sample, the absorbance of visible light increased, promoting light absorption during photocatalytic degradation process. In further step, the band gap energies of the photocatalysts were determined by using Tauc plots as 2.10 eV and 2.04 eV for FeTiO3 and FeTiO3/CQDs(III), respectively (Fig. 7(b)). It could be stated that the decoration with carbon quantum dots enhanced optical absorption characteristics of the bare perovskite. In addition, PL analysis was performed to examine the separation and recombination behaviors of photoexcited carriers, and the relevant spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. As depicted in the Fig. 7(c), in the spectrum of FeTiO3 sample, three main peaks were identified at 430 nm, 460 nm and 490 nm, which were attributed to the degree of electron-hole combination, the existence of oxygen-included phased on the surface and the surface defects, respectively [45,46]. In terms of FeTiO3/CQDs(III) sample, the emission peaks appeared at about 460 nm and 490 nm and the peak intensities were obviously lower than the bare photocatalyst, indicating that the recombination of electron-hole pairs was hindered in the CQDs-decorated FeTiO3 sample. This was due to fact that the combination of CQDs with FeTiO3 enhanced visible light absorption ability, accelerated the electron transfer rate, and limited the process of electron and hole recombination, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic degradation.

The photocatalytic efficiencies of synthesized FeTiO3, FeTiO3/CQDs catalysts were first evaluated by the degradation of CIP as a model pollutant (Fig. 8 ). Before the light-irradiated tests, the adsorption efficiencies of the samples in dark environment were investigated and the results (inset of Fig. 8(a)) indicated that the absorption–desorption balance between the antibiotic molecules and the catalyst was achieved after 30 min of adsorption process. The adsorptive removal performances were calculated as 29.4 % and 33.1 % for FeTiO3 and FeTiO3/CQDs, respectively. The pristine FeTiO3 catalyst showed a moderate adsorption affinity towards CIP molecules, which might be due to electrostatic interactions between iron and negatively charged π-electron cloud of ciprofloxacin [47]. It was also observed that the introduction of different contents of CQDs slightly enhanced the removal efficiency of the hybrid catalysts, which was attributed to the adsorption affinity of carbon quantum dots towards CIP molecules. The surface functional groups of CQDs might be involved in the adsorption of molecules. Additionally, the conjugated structures in CQDs might be beneficial to attach the CIP molecules via π–π interactions, enabling enhanced adsorption and photocatalysis [48]. Similar phenomenon was observed by Di et al. [49] that the increasing ratio of CQDs improved the dye adsorption affinity of the Bi2WO4/CQDs composite. On the other hand, under visible light irradiation, the photocatalytic efficiency of the pristine FeTiO3 increased from 36.5 % to 68.4 % by using the FeTiO3/CQDs(III) catalyst, indicating that the modification with CQDs significantly influenced the degradation performance. The FeTiO3/CQDs(I) (5 mL) and FeTiO3/CQDs(II) (12 mL) heterostructure catalysts exhibited lower CIP decomposition efficiency, which were calculated as 61.3 % and 64.2 %, respectively. This could be due to the fact that low content of CQDs was not sufficient to improve light absorption rate, enhance the separation rate of the photoexcited species and increase oxygen vacancies. The changes of the CIP concentration versus illumination time over the catalysts were investigated with pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the calculated kinetic rate constants (k, min−1) were shown in Fig. 8(c). The reaction rate constants of FeTiO3 increased gradually from 0.0009 min−1 to 0.0061 min−1 for FeTiO3/CQDs(III), respectively. The highest rate constant was obtained for FeTiO3/CQDs(III), which was almost 6.49 times higher than that of raw FeTiO3. These results confirmed that the decoration of FeTiO3 by CQDs promoted the degradation of CIP under visible light illumination due to enhanced optical and structural features.

In order to obtain an ideal result out of a photocatalytic remediation system, catalyst dosage should be considered as one of the important parameters. Considering this, different amounts of FeTiO3/CQDs (0.5–1 g/L) were loaded in a series of reactions to determine the highest photocatalytic activity (Fig. 9(a)). As expected, the catalyst dosage of 0.5 g/L resulted in the lowest performance, which was calculated as 68.4 % at the end of 120 min of irradiation. The effectiveness of CIP decomposition was concurrently enhanced by increment in the catalyst amount, wherein the photodegradation efficiencies reached 87.7 % and 87.9 % for the dosages of 0.75 g/L and 1 g/L, respectively. The increase in catalyst dosage provided higher degradation which enhanced interaction of target CIP molecules with active surface sites of the catalyst. The catalyst dosage of 0.75 g/L exhibited almost similar performance with the dosage of 1 g/L, so the optimum catalyst dosage was chosen as 0.75 g/L for subsequent catalytic experiments.

The influence of initial antibiotic concentration in a concentration range of 5–15 mg/L on its decomposition was investigated. As shown in Fig. 9(c), the highest removal (96.6 % degradation) was attained at initial concentration of 5 mg/L within 120 min. At initial CIP concentration of 10 mg/L, the photodegradation efficiency (%) was found to be 87.7 %, while the decomposition rate significantly decreased to 71.5 % when the CIP concentration increased to 15 mg/L. The kinetic rate constants decreased gradually with increasing concentration and the values were calculated as 0.0230 min−1, 0.0132 min−1 and 0.0068 min−1 for the initial CIP concentrations of 5 mg/L, 10 mg/L and 15 mg/L, respectively. Since higher pollutant concentrations requires higher amount of oxidative radical species, the increasing pollutant concentration decreases the oxidant to pollutant ratio, leading to lower photo-degradation performance [50]. On the other hand, following the Langmuir's theory of adsorption, the adsorption sites on the hybrid catalyst might be occupied by higher amounts of antibiotic molecules, resulting in a decreased removal efficiency [51,52].

Solution pH influences surface charge features of adsorbent as well as adsorbate molecules which in turn affects photocatalytic performance. Thus, the photodegradation efficiency of CIP at different solution pH in the range of 3–10 was studied, and the results were presented in Fig. 10(a). The CIP decomposition rate by using FeTiO3/CQDs was found highest as 87 % when the solution pH was 6.1. The photocatalytic activity slightly decreased to 81 % with a decrease in pH from 6.1 to 3.0; while the antibiotic degradation significantly decreased to 64.6 % at alkaline medium (pH = 10). It is known that ciprofloxacin molecule has two dissociation constants as pK a1 = 6.1 and pK a2 = 8.7, which means that CIP exists in cationic form (CIP+) at pH <6.1, zwitterionic form (CIP±) at pH ∼ 6.1–8.7 and anionic form (CIP−) at pH higher than 8.7 [53]. Considering the pK a values of CIP (Fig. 10(c)), the repulsion forces might be dominant between CIP− molecules and negatively charged groups of the catalyst which inhibited the catalytic activity at basic conditions. On the other hand, the photodegradation efficiency was found stable at pH in the range of 3–6.1, revealing excellent pH adaptability of the FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalysts in photocatalytic processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radical scavenger tests and degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The radical trapping tests were conducted for the CIP degradation over FeTiO3/CQDs to elucidate the significance of generated active radical and hole species in the photocatalytic process. BQ, EDTA-2Na, and IPA were used as superoxide anion (•O2 −), photogenerated hole (h+) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) trappings, respectively [54]. As depicted in Fig. 11(a), the degradation efficiency was significantly decreased from 87.7 % to 33.9 % in the presence of BQ, indicating that •O2 − species played an indispensable part in the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. On the other hand, the CIP degradation performance was found as 39 % in the presence of EDTA-2Na, implying that the photogenerated holes were also important in the photocatalytic reaction. When IPA was added into the reaction, a lower level of reduction in the photocatalytic decomposition rate was observed, demonstrating that the effect of •OH on the degradation was smaller than those of •O2 − and h+ species. Fig. 11(b) shows the kinetic rate constants for each condition. Therefore, the ranked order of contribution of three radicals for the degradation of CIP molecule was •O2 − > h+ > •OH.

In addition, in order to explore the photocatalytic reaction mechanism, the band positions of the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of FeTiO3/CQDs were examined by using XPS analysis which informs about the total density of states of VB [49]. The valence band of FeTiO3/CQDs(III) was measured by XPS spectrum. As depicted in Fig. 11(c), the VB edge potential was found at about 2.3 eV. According to the formula of ECB = EVB − Eg, the corresponding CB edge potential was calculated as +0.26 eV (Eg = 2.04 eV). Thus, the valence band of FeTiO3/CQDs (2.3 eV) can be enough to oxidize water molecules to yield •OH radicals (•OH/OH−, +2.1 eV). However, the CB potential of FeTiO3/CQDs (+0.26 eV) is not enough to reduce O2 into •O2 − (− 0.33 eV). Based on the experimental and characterization results, the CIP degradation mechanism over the FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalyst is illustrated in Fig. 11(d). Upon irradiation by visible light, the photo-excited electrons on the valence band of FeTiO3 were transferred to its conduction band, leaving holes in the valence band of FeTiO3. Then, the photogenerated electrons on the CB tended to move the surface of CQDs to produce the superoxide radicals (e− (CQDs) + O2 → •O2). The trapping of electrons by CQDs hindered the recombination and promoted the separation of the charge carriers, which was one of the enhancing features of CQDs-based hybrid photocatalyst. More importantly, since the conduction band potential of the pristine FeTiO3 was not enough to generate superoxide radicals, the presence of CQDs provided the production of super oxide radicals which were found as the main active species to degrade CIP molecule. Meanwhile, the accumulated holes in the valence band of FeTiO3 could directly oxidize CIP into harmless byproducts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation the roles of oxidants in the degradation
ce:section.ce:para: In order to find out the Fenton effect on the antibiotic degradation by using FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalyst, the photo-Fenton reactions with H2O2 or PMS were carried out. The photo-Fenton degradation efficiencies at different oxidant concentrations were depicted in Fig. 12 . The results clearly indicated that the photo-degradation efficiency of CIP (87.7 % degradation) greatly enhanced in the presence of both oxidants. In the presence of H2O2, the photo-Fenton decomposition rates were obtained as 90.4 %, 96.1 % and 98.4 % for H2O2 concentrations of 3 mM, 5 mM and 10 mM, respectively. Interestingly, the photo-Fenton degradation rates achieved using 5 mM and 10 mM concentrations of H2O2 were quite similar, revealing that the optimum H2O2 concentration can be selected as 5 mM. Similarly, the CIP degradation was markedly increased in the presence of sulfate radical agent. After 2 h of light irradiation, the increase in PMS concentration from 3 mM to 5 mM enhanced the decomposition from 97.2 % to 98.2 %; while complete degradation was noticed when the PMS concentration was 10 mM under identical reaction conditions. The increasing trend in the CIP degradation efficiency was small when the PMS concentration exceeded 3 mM. There has been no phenomenon that excessive PMS causes scavenging of free radicals and inhibits the catalytic reaction [55]. The obtained results revealed that excessive PMS might be adsorbed on the catalyst blocking the active surface sites, thereby influencing the degradation performance. Considering the economical aspect of the process, 3.0 mM PMS can be chosen as optimum concentration in the degradation of CIP over the FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalytic system. Summarizing, the above results demonstrated that the combination of visible light and heterogeneous Fenton system created a synergy for effective degradation of fluoroquinolone antibiotic.

The recyclability and stability of a photocatalyst are important features in practical applications. To evaluate the reusability and stability of the FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalyst, recycling tests were performed for the photodegradation of CIP under visible light irradiation. In Fig. 13(a), the photocatalytic decomposition performance of CIP by FeTiO3/CQDs was stable after nine consecutive cycles and no apparent deactivation was observed, thus implying that the as-prepared hybrid catalyst had high stability and reusability for the photocatalytic degradation of pharmaceuticals from wastewater. Additionally, the stability of crystalline structure was investigated by comparing the XRD patterns of FeTiO3/CQDs before and after final cycle. As shown in Fig. 13(b), the characteristic diffraction peaks of rhombohedral FeTiO3 still remained and no other material peaks were observed in the XRD pattern of the spent catalyst, demonstrating that the catalyst structure was not destroyed during degradation reaction. The results verified that the FeTiO3/CQDs catalyst with remarkable stability has efficient application prospects for the remediation of environmental pollutants.

In summary, novel FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalysts have been fabricated for the first time via a facile ultrasonication followed by impregnation processes. The characterization results demonstrated that the carbon quantum dots were well dispersed over the surface of sphere-like FeTiO3 via tight junctions which were confirmed by XPS analysis. Optical tests confirmed that extended visible light absorption and hindered recombination rate of charge pairs were achieved after the introduction of CQDs into the perovskite structure. More importantly, the efficient charge transfer feature of CQDs provided additional boost for photocatalytic performance. Under visible light irradiation, the photocatalytic activity of the bare FeTiO3 for the degradation of ciprofloxacin antibiotic greatly increased in which the kinetic constant rate of the hybrid catalyst was 6.4 times-higher than the bare FeTiO3 perovskite. The radical trapping tests indicated that the reactive species of •O2 − and h+ played major role in the decomposition process of the pharmaceutical pollutant. Moreover, in the photo-Fenton catalytic experiments, remarkable degradation efficiencies were obtained in the presence of H2O2 and PMS as Fenton agents. Finally, the FeTiO3/CQDs hybrid catalyst displayed superior stability until nine catalytic cycles. This study not only demonstrates the role of CQDs in modulating the photocatalytic activity of FeTiO3 photocatalyst, but also provides a feasible and eco-friendly route to treat pharmaceutical-based wastewater.

Esra Bilgin Simsek: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Özlem Tuna: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft.
